title: L'heure et la routine quotidienne
description: Telling the time and talking about your daily routine with reflexive verbs.

cards.notes: The standard way to ask the time. Literally *what hour is it?* The **h** in *heure* is silent.
Use **il est** + number + *heure(s)* to tell the time. *Une heure* (singular), but *deux heures*, *trois heures*, ... (plural).
*Il est trois heures **et demie*** — it is half past three. *Demie* agrees with the feminine *heure*.
*Il est trois heures **et quart*** — it is quarter past three.
*Il est trois heures **moins le quart*** — it is quarter to three. *Moins* means *minus*.
*Il est midi* — it is noon (12:00). No need for *heures*: just *midi*.
*Il est minuit* — it is midnight (00:00). Like *midi*, it stands alone.
*Le matin, je me lève tôt* — in the morning, I get up early.
*Le soir, je me couche tard* — in the evening, I go to bed late.
A **reflexive** verb. *Je **me** réveille* — I wake up. The *se* is the reflexive pronoun in the infinitive.
Reflexive. Note the accent shift: *je me **lève*** (grave accent), but *nous nous **levons*** (no accent).
Reflexive. The **h** is silent, so *se* elides to *s'*: *je **m'**habille*.
Reflexive. *Elle **se** couche à dix heures* — she goes to bed at ten o'clock.
Literally *to take the little lunch*. *Je prends le petit-déjeuner le matin.*

steps.title: Telling the time
steps.body:
# Quelle heure est-il ?

To ask the time in French:

> **Quelle heure est-il ?** — What time is it?

To answer, use **il est** + the hour + *heure(s)*:

| French | English |
|---|---|
| Il est **une** heure | It is one o'clock |
| Il est **deux** heures | It is two o'clock |
| Il est **dix** heures | It is ten o'clock |

Note: *une heure* is **singular** (one hour), but from *deux* up it's *heures* (plural).

For the half- and quarter-hours, add a short phrase after the hour:

- *Il est trois heures **et demie**.* — It is half past three.
- *Il est trois heures **et quart**.* — It is quarter past three.
- *Il est trois heures **moins le quart**.* — It is quarter to three.

And two special words with no *heures*:

- *Il est **midi**.* — It is noon (12:00).
- *Il est **minuit**.* — It is midnight (00:00).

exercise.prompt: Match each French time expression with its English meaning.
Match each reflexive verb with its meaning.

steps.title: Reflexive (pronominal) verbs
steps.body:
## Verbs you do *to yourself*

Reflexive verbs describe actions you do to yourself — waking up, getting dressed, going to bed. In the dictionary they carry a **se** in front of the infinitive: *se lever*, *se coucher*, *s'habiller*.

When you conjugate them, the *se* changes to match the subject. Here is **se lever** (to get up):

| Subject | Pronoun | Verb |
|---|---|---|
| je | **me** | je **me lève** |
| tu | **te** | tu **te lèves** |
| il / elle | **se** | il **se lève** |
| nous | **nous** | nous **nous levons** |
| vous | **vous** | vous **vous levez** |
| ils / elles | **se** | ils **se lèvent** |

**Accent trap:** *se lever* takes a grave accent (è) in most forms — *je me **lève**, tu te **lèves**, il se **lève**, ils se **lèvent*** — but **not** in *nous **levons*** and *vous **levez***.

**Elision:** before a vowel or silent *h*, *me/te/se* become *m'/t'/s'*: *je **m'**habille*, *tu **t'**habilles*.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I get up at seven o'clock.*
accept: Je me lève à sept heures.
je me lève à sept heures
Je me leve a sept heures.
exercise.hint: *Je me* + the *lève* form + *à sept heures*. Remember the grave accent on *lève*.
distractors: Je me lever à sept heures.

steps.title: Build: In the morning I get up at six

exercise.prompt: Arrange to mean: *In the morning I get up at six o'clock.*
exercise.hint: Time phrase first (*Le matin*), then subject + reflexive pronoun + verb + *à* + the hour.

exercise.prompt: Which clock shows *il est trois heures et demie* (half past three)?
exercise.hint: *Et demie* means half past — the minute hand points straight down at the 6.

steps.title: Putting time and routine together
steps.body:
## Describing your day

Now combine the two halves: a **time phrase** plus a **reflexive verb** gives you a full daily-routine sentence.

Use *à* + the hour to say *at* a given time:

- *Le matin, je me lève **à sept heures**.* — In the morning, I get up at seven o'clock.
- *Je prends le petit-déjeuner **à huit heures**.* — I have breakfast at eight o'clock.
- *Le soir, je me couche **à dix heures et demie**.* — In the evening, I go to bed at half past ten.

A typical order is: *(time phrase) + subject + reflexive pronoun + verb + à + hour*.

Try describing your own day out loud:

> *Le matin, je me réveille, je me lève, et je m'habille. Le soir, je me couche.*

You now have the pieces to talk about an entire day in French. Keep going — you're nearly through A1!

exercise.prompt: How do you say *It is noon*?
exercise.hint: Use *Il est* + the special word for noon. No *heures* needed.

steps.title: Build: She goes to bed at ten

exercise.prompt: Arrange to mean: *She goes to bed at ten o'clock.*
exercise.hint: Subject + reflexive pronoun *se* + the *couche* form + *à* + the hour.

steps.title: I have breakfast in the morning

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I have breakfast in the morning.*
accept: Je prends le petit-déjeuner le matin.
je prends le petit-déjeuner le matin
Je prends le petit-dejeuner le matin.
distractors: Je prend le petit-déjeuner le matin.
Je prends le petit-déjeuner le soir.
Je me prends le petit-déjeuner le matin.
